title: Ashwagandha warm milk
subtitle: An stress-supporting herb bedtime preparation
excerpt: Ashwagandha root powder simmered in warm whole milk with a little honey. The traditional Ayurvedic preparation for stress recovery and adrenal fatigue. A calming, mildly sedating drink best taken before bed.
sourceNotes: Ayurvedic Pharmacopoeia of India, Withania somnifera reference entry: the milk-based ashwagandha preparations (ashwagandha ksheera paka) and the stress-supporting herb nerve-calming herb tradition. the World Health Organisation Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Vol. 1, Radix Withaniae Somniferae: the modern evidence summary and safety review.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Ashwagandha root powder stores 2 years in a sealed tin, cool and dark. Prepare each cup fresh.

glossaryTerms.definition: A herb traditionally described as supporting the body's response to physical and mental stress. The term was introduced in Soviet pharmacology in the 1940s; ashwagandha is the best-researched Ayurvedic adaptogen in modern clinical literature.
Steroidal lactones unique to Withania somnifera (ashwagandha), considered the principal active constituents. They are associated with the adaptogenic and nervine-sedative actions.

content.text: : traditionally used by those recovering from illness, under stress, or dealing with fatigue. Its

are the main active compounds studied. The preparation is a simmered root powder in warm milk. Fat helps absorption. The warm milk also promotes sleep. A pinch of honey and black pepper completes the drink. Taken before bed, it is warming and mildly sedating.

attrs.title: Important safety notes
attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Pregnancy. Do not use ashwagandha in pregnancy. It is avoided throughout pregnancy in the Ayurvedic tradition and modern safety reviews.

Thyroid. Ashwagandha can raise thyroid hormone levels. Readers on levothyroxine or other thyroid hormone replacement must consult their prescriber before regular use.

Autoimmune conditions. Ashwagandha has immune-modulating activity. Readers with lupus, rheumatoid arthritis, multiple sclerosis, or other autoimmune conditions should consult a herbalist before use.

Solanaceae allergy. Ashwagandha is in the nightshade family (Solanaceae). Readers with a known nightshade sensitivity should approach with caution.

Paediatric use. Not for children under twelve without specialist advice.

Fatigue that is severe, progressive, or associated with unexplained weight loss, night sweats, persistent fever, or significant mood change needs medical assessment, not herbal management.

items.prepNote: ashwagandha root powder (not capsule powder); about half a teaspoon
or an unsweetened oat or nut milk; full-fat milk gives the best result
optional, to sweeten; add after removing from heat
a tiny pinch, freshly ground; piperine improves withanolide absorption

content.text: Combine the ashwagandha powder and a small splash of cold milk in a small saucepan. Whisk to a smooth paste (this prevents lumping when the remaining milk goes in). Add the rest of the milk and the black pepper. Heat over medium-low heat, whisking regularly, until the milk is steaming and just below a simmer. Do not boil. Simmer gently for 3-4 minutes, whisking occasionally. Remove from heat. Add honey if using. The preparation will be pale cream-gold and slightly earthy in smell and taste.

One cup (containing 3 g root powder) before bed, once daily. the World Health Organisation reference entry reference dose is 3-6 g dried root daily; one bedtime cup sits at the lower end of this range. Take for up to 3 months continuously, then take a 1-month break before continuing (the stress-supporting herb tradition recommends cycled use). Paediatric use: not for children under twelve.
Prepare each cup fresh. Ashwagandha root powder stores 2 years in a sealed tin, cool and dark.
When not to use this remedy

In pregnancy. On thyroid medication without prescriber approval. With autoimmune disease without herbalist guidance. Severe fatigue with unexplained weight loss or night sweats needs medical investigation.
